title: How to Bake Sourdough Biscuits on Silicone Mats
meta_description: Learn how to bake perfect sourdough biscuits using a silicone baking mat. Get tips, step‑by‑step instructions, and why the non‑stick mat is a game‑changer.
html:
Whether you’re a sourdough enthusiast or just love flaky biscuits, the secret to consistent results lies in the surface you bake on. A sourdough biscuits baking mat provides a truly non‑stick, reusable platform that eliminates the need for parchment and cuts cleanup to zero. Below is a practical guide that walks you through every step—from feeding your starter to pulling golden biscuits off the mat.

Why a Silicone Baking Mat Beats Parchment for Biscuits

Silicone baking mats are heat‑resistant, flexible, and have a perfectly smooth surface. When you place dough on a silicone mat, the heat transfers evenly, giving biscuits a uniform rise and a crisp bottom without the risk of sticking. Because the mat is reusable, you also avoid the waste and cost of parchment each bake.

Preparing the Sourdough Starter

To make sourdough biscuits, you need a starter that’s active and bubbly. Here’s a quick refresher:

  1. Discard half of your starter (about ½ cup).
  2. Feed the remaining starter with equal parts flour and water (e.g., ½ cup flour, ½ cup water).
  3. Cover loosely and let sit at room temperature for 4‑6 hours, or until it doubles in size and shows plenty of bubbles.

When the starter passes the “float test” (a spoonful floats in water), it’s ready to be incorporated into the biscuit dough.

Mixing the Biscuit Dough on a Silicone Baking Mat

Using the silicone mat as your mixing surface keeps the dough from sticking while you work. Follow these steps for a tender, flaky biscuit:

  • Dry ingredients: In a large bowl, whisk together 2 cups all‑purpose flour, 1 tbsp baking powder, ½ tsp baking soda, and ½ tsp salt.
  • Wet ingredients: In a separate bowl, combine ½ cup cold unsalted butter (cut into cubes), ¾ cup buttermilk, and ¼ cup active sourdough starter. Stir until the butter is coated but still visible in small flakes.
  • Combine: Pour the wet mixture onto the silicone baking mat. Sprinkle the dry ingredients over it and gently fold with a spatula until the dough just comes together. Over‑mixing will develop gluten and make the biscuits tough.
  • Rest: Let the dough rest on the mat for 5‑10 minutes. This relaxes the gluten and lets the butter melt slightly, contributing to a flaky texture.

While the dough rests, preheat your oven to 425 °F (220 °C). The heat should be hot enough to create steam inside the biscuits, which is essential for lift.

Shaping and Baking on the Silicone Baking Mat

When the dough is rested, turn it out onto the silicone mat. Lightly flour the mat if your dough is very sticky, but most of the time the non‑stick surface is enough. Here’s how to shape the biscuits:

  1. Pat the dough into a ½‑inch thick rectangle.
  2. Using a 2‑inch biscuit cutter (or a floured glass), cut circles and place them directly on the silicone mat.
  3. Gather the scraps, reshape, and cut more biscuits until all dough is used.

Arrange the biscuits close together (they’ll expand) or spaced apart if you prefer crispier edges. Slide the mat onto a baking sheet for stability, then bake for 12‑15 minutes, or until the tops are golden brown and the bottoms feel firm to the touch.

Cleaning and Maintaining Your Silicone Baking Mat

One of the biggest benefits of a silicone baking mat is the zero‑scrub cleanup. After the biscuits have cooled, simply let the mat sit until it’s warm, then wipe it with a damp cloth. For stubborn residue, a quick wash in warm, soapy water or a short run through the dishwasher (top rack only) does the trick. Dry it flat, and it’s ready for the next bake.

Tips for Perfect Sourdough Biscuits Every Time

Even with a great mat, a few small adjustments can elevate your biscuits:

  • Cold butter: Keep butter as cold as possible until it’s incorporated. The cold pockets melt during baking, creating layers.
  • Buttermilk temperature: Use cold buttermilk to keep the dough chilled, which helps with flakiness.
  • Don’t over‑mix: Stop mixing once the flour is just hydrated. A few streaks of flour are fine.
  • Steam boost: Place a shallow pan of water on the lower oven rack while the biscuits bake. The extra steam encourages a higher rise.

With these tricks and the reliable performance of a silicone baking mat, you’ll produce biscuits that are crisp on the bottom, tender inside, and infused with the subtle tang of sourdough.

FAQ

Can I use a silicone baking mat for high‑heat recipes?

Yes. Most silicone mats are rated for up to 480 °F (250 °C). Baking biscuits at 425 °F is well within the safe range.

Do I need to grease the silicone mat before baking biscuits?

No. The mat’s non‑stick surface eliminates the need for oil or butter. If you prefer extra browning, you can lightly spray the tops of the biscuits with oil before baking.

How long will the silicone baking mat last?

When cared for properly—avoiding sharp utensils and washing gently—a high‑quality silicone baking mat can last for several years, handling thousands of bakings without losing its non‑stick properties.
